channel.title: Simon's Newsletter Digest
channel.description: Auto-summarised newsletter entries, powered by Claude Haiku

item.title: ☕ Another element [14/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Chesney the kangaroo escaped his 8-foot enclosure at Sunshine Farm in Necedah, Wisconsin after being spooked by stray dogs. He remained on the run for 3 days. His keeper Debbie Marland walked approximately 37,000 steps per day searching for him.
item.keywords: kangaroo escape, Wisconsin, animal incident

item.title: ☕ Another element [13/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The NCAA Men's Basketball Final Four is set: Illinois vs. UConn and Michigan vs. Arizona, with both games on Saturday. The women's Final Four is decided tonight, with the women's championship on Sunday and men's championship on Monday.
item.keywords: March Madness, NCAA, Final Four, basketball

item.title: ☕ Another element [12/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: NASA's Artemis II mission launch window opens Wednesday evening after years of delays and over $44 billion in spending. Four astronauts will travel to the moon and back in approximately 10 days. If conditions are unfavorable, additional launch windows are available daily through April 6.
item.keywords: NASA, Artemis II, moon mission, space exploration

item.title: ☕ Another element [11/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Apple celebrates its 50th anniversary with a major event at its Cupertino headquarters on Wednesday. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman hinted that Paul McCartney could headline the celebration finale.
item.keywords: Apple, 50th anniversary, Cupertino, Paul McCartney

item.title: ☕ Another element [10/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The March jobs report will be released Friday, with analysts expecting approximately 55,000 new jobs after February's decline of 92,000. February's JOLTS report releases tomorrow and initial jobless claims data Thursday. The labor market shows signs of recovery after a difficult February.
item.keywords: March jobs report, employment data, labor market, JOLTS

item.title: ☕ Another element [9/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: American pharmaceutical companies are increasingly leveraging China's investments in research and low trial costs for drug discovery. Biotech investor Chen Yu noted companies can obtain clinical proof of concept in China before expensive US development. However, potential American legislation could restrict large drugmakers from paying China for assets rather than acquiring US startups.
item.keywords: pharmaceutical industry, China, drug discovery, clinical trials, regulatory concerns

item.title: ☕ Another element [8/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Eli Lilly announced a $2.75 billion deal with Hong Kong-based AI drug discovery startup Insilico Medicine. Insilico receives $115 million upfront with potential to earn the full value through regulatory milestones, sales achievements, and royalties. Lilly obtains exclusive rights to an Insilico-developed GLP-1 drug for diabetes.
item.keywords: Eli Lilly, Insilico Medicine, AI drug discovery, GLP-1, $2.75 billion deal

item.title: ☕ Another element [7/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Zillow launched a new 'Preview' feature allowing brokerages to showcase homes as pre-listings before market listing. Five initial brokers including Keller Williams and Remax participated starting March 17; 24 additional firms have since joined. Competing platforms Homes.com, Realtor.com, and ComeHome.com launched their own pre-market offerings, many requiring exclusivity agreements.
item.keywords: Zillow, real estate, pre-listing, Keller Williams, Remax, home buying

item.title: ☕ Another element [6/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The US permitted a Russian oil tanker named Anatoly Kolodkin carrying 650,000 barrels of Urals crude to enter Cuba despite an oil blockade imposed since January. The blockade has worsened Cuba's energy crisis causing multiple power outages and deteriorating medical conditions. Trump stated he had 'no problem' with the tanker because 'they have to survive.'
item.keywords: Russia, Cuba, oil tanker, US blockade, energy crisis, Trump

item.title: ☕ Another element [5/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Yemen's Houthis launched missiles at Israel on Saturday, marking their first direct participation in the Iran war. The US Central Command reported that 2,500 Marines trained in amphibious landings arrived in the Middle East aboard the USS Tripoli. President Trump indicated preferences to 'take the oil' from Iran and is considering military operations to extract uranium.
item.keywords: Houthis, Yemen, Iran war, Israel, US Marines, USS Tripoli, Trump

item.title: ☕ Another element [4/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Middle East produces approximately 9% of global aluminum supply but faces vulnerability due to energy-intensive extraction processes. A Qatar aluminum plant jointly owned by QatarEnergy and Norsk Hydro reduced capacity to 60% due to natural gas supply issues. India's Hindalco Industries invoked force majeure clauses citing Iran war-related gas supply disruptions. Aluminum prices have risen about 4% since the war started on February 28, compounded by Trump's 50% tariffs on the metal.
item.keywords: aluminum prices, Middle East production, natural gas supply, Strait of Hormuz, force majeure, tariffs

item.title: ☕ Another element [3/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps conducted intentional drone and missile attacks on aluminum producers in US-allied nations. Emirates Global Aluminum in Abu Dhabi and Aluminum Bahrain (Alba) both suffered significant damage, with several employees injured at the UAE facility. Alba had previously suspended 19% of production capacity due to Strait of Hormuz supply disruptions.
item.keywords: Iran war, aluminum production, UAE, Bahrain, drone attacks, supply disruption

item.title: ☕ Another element [2/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Major stock indices closed with significant losses for the fifth consecutive week: Nasdaq down 9.87% YTD, S&P down 6.96% YTD, and Dow down 6.03% YTD. Alphabet finished down approximately 9% last week despite publishing research on new AI data compression methods. Markets will be closed on Friday for Good Friday.
item.keywords: stock market decline, Nasdaq, S&P 500, Dow Jones, correction territory, Alphabet

item.title: ☕ Another element [1/14]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: KitKat reported that a truck carrying over 400,000 units was stolen after leaving a factory in central Italy on March 29, heading to Poland. The company initially warned of potential Easter shortages, but later confirmed supply would not be affected and there were no consumer safety concerns. The stolen shipment consisted of Formula 1 branded KitKats.
item.keywords: KitKat theft, Italy, Easter shortage, Formula 1, supply chain

item.title: 學術式如何在美股找尋到分析「十倍股」，一個數據的增長是非常重要?
item.author: 中環十一少 <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: The email discusses academic theories and frameworks for identifying "10-bagger" stocks (stocks that return 10x) in the U.S. stock market. The author emphasizes that data growth is critically important for long-term investment success, and presents academic principles as a systematic approach to finding profitable stock investments, contrasting this with urban legends about following high-growth tech stocks or copying major traders.
item.keywords: 10-bagger stocks, U.S. stock market analysis, data growth metrics, long-term investment strategy, academic investment theory

item.title: 一周美股前瞻｜復活節前四日市 Nike運動品牌同RH奢侈家居雙雄 ISM製造業PMI升至52 本周業績：NKE、BYND、MKC、PRGS、LW（3月30日至4月3日）
item.author: MHK TECH <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: US stock market preview for the week of March 30 - April 3, 2026. Key highlights include four trading days before Easter, Nike and RH (luxury home furnishings) as major earnings drivers, ISM manufacturing PMI rising to 52, and scheduled earnings reports from NKE, BYND, MKC, PRGS, and LW. Federal Reserve Chair Powell and Williams are scheduled to speak, which will influence policy expectations.
item.keywords: US stock market outlook, Nike earnings, RH earnings, ISM manufacturing PMI 52, Easter holiday trading, Federal Reserve speakers, BYND MKC PRGS LW earnings

item.title: 本周美股前瞻｜標指五連跌逼近修正 VIX破30恐慌蔓延 特價再起 四隻本周當炒股（3月30日）
item.author: MHK TECH <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Weekly U.S. stock market outlook for March 30, 2026. The S&P 500 is approaching correction territory after five consecutive declines, with VIX breaking above 30 indicating heightened market panic. The column reviews previous week's stock picks (March 23) which underperformed significantly, with an average decline of 6.31% versus NASDAQ's 4.76% drop, resulting in zero wins and four losses—the worst weekly performance since the column's inception. IonQ (IONQ) was the biggest drag, plummeting 12.94% from $31.60 to $27.51.
item.keywords: S&P 500 correction, VIX above 30, market panic, IONQ stock decline, stock picks performance, March 2026 market outlook

item.title: 焦點22:30｜30.03.2026｜逾五百宏福苑居民聯署倡放寬上樓規定、庭上揭衞志樑曾嫌英人工低欲回流香港、鄒幸彤解讀改朝換代指非針對中共領導 [3/3]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Zou Xingtong (鄒幸彤) provided an interpretation clarifying that 'change of era/regime' (改朝換代) does not specifically target the leadership of the Chinese Communist Party. This statement addresses concerns about the meaning and scope of such political discourse.
item.keywords: Zou Xingtong, political interpretation, regime change, Chinese Communist Party

item.title: 焦點22:30｜30.03.2026｜逾五百宏福苑居民聯署倡放寬上樓規定、庭上揭衞志樑曾嫌英人工低欲回流香港、鄒幸彤解讀改朝換代指非針對中共領導 [2/3]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Court proceedings revealed that Wei Zhiliang (衞志樑) previously complained about low wages in the UK and expressed a desire to return to Hong Kong. This personal disclosure emerged during legal testimony.
item.keywords: Wei Zhiliang, court testimony, UK wages, Hong Kong return

item.title: 焦點22:30｜30.03.2026｜逾五百宏福苑居民聯署倡放寬上樓規定、庭上揭衞志樑曾嫌英人工低欲回流香港、鄒幸彤解讀改朝換代指非針對中共領導 [1/3]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Over 500 residents of Hong Kong Public Housing Estate (宏福苑) signed a petition calling for relaxed regulations on moving into public housing units. The petition represents a significant community effort to address housing policy concerns.
item.keywords: Hong Kong public housing, residents petition, housing regulations, 宏福苑

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [7/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: The Verge discusses the MacBook Air as Apple's most imitated product and explores how the original Macintosh fundamentally changed computer design and functionality.
item.keywords: MacBook Air, Macintosh, computer design, product influence

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [6/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: Articles examine Steve Jobs' 10-year return to Apple during which the company produced influential products including iMacs, iBooks, and iPods, as well as the critical importance of Steve Wozniak's Apple II to the company's survival.
item.keywords: Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, Apple II, iMac, product innovation

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [5/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: Coverage includes Apple's long-running manufacturing relationship with FoxConn, detailed in an excerpt from Patrick McGee's book 'Apple in China'.
item.keywords: Apple FoxConn, Apple manufacturing, Apple in China, supply chain

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [4/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: The Verge is covering Apple's ongoing antitrust legal battles, particularly surrounding the App Store and iPhone empire, with international legal pushback occurring globally.
item.keywords: Apple antitrust, App Store antitrust, iPhone legal battles, regulatory pressure

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [3/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: The Verge has compiled a ranking of 50 of the best Apple products throughout history, from the original iPhone to GarageBand, inviting readers to participate in ranking them.
item.keywords: Apple products ranking, top 50 Apple products, iPhone, GarageBand

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [2/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: The Verge is running a limited-time Flash Sale offering new annual subscribers 50% off access to exclusive reviews, reporting, and analysis.
item.keywords: The Verge subscription, Flash Sale, 50% discount

item.title: Looking back at Apple’s first 50 years [1/7]
item.author: The Verge <subscriptions@theverge.com>
item.description: Apple Computer Company was founded on April 1st, 1976, and is now celebrating its 50th anniversary as one of the world's most valuable companies. Over five decades, Apple has shaped technology and culture through iconic products like the iPad, iPhone, iPod, and MacBook, while experiencing both triumphs and failures across different eras of leadership.
item.keywords: Apple 50th anniversary, Apple history, Steve Jobs, iconic Apple products, tech industry impact

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [8/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: Apple App Store review processes are becoming overwhelmed by a surge in app submissions, creating bottlenecks in the update pipeline. The review system cannot scale with submission rates, creating a systemic problem with no clear resolution.
item.keywords: App Store review process, app submission backlog, scaling challenges, app updates

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [7/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: Apple is offering stock options to employees in an effort to stem talent losses to OpenAI and Meta, indicating growing competition for AI engineering talent among major tech companies.
item.keywords: Apple talent retention, stock options, OpenAI recruitment, tech talent competition

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [6/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: China's AI talent gap—not just model performance—may be the critical competitive issue going forward. While focus has centered on Chinese AI models, the underlying talent shortage could be a larger structural challenge in the long term.
item.keywords: China AI talent, AI competition, talent gap, competitive advantage

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [5/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: Internal conflict between Dario and Daniella Amodei versus Sam Altman and Greg Brockman at OpenAI led to the creation of Anthropic, with roots tracing back a decade. The schism involved egos, strategic disagreements, and double-dealing that spiraled into a major corporate rift. Both companies are now valued in the hundreds of billions and racing toward public offerings while competing in AI development.
item.keywords: OpenAI Anthropic rivalry, Dario Amodei, Sam Altman, internal conflict origins, IPO preparation

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [4/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: Project Hail Mary surpassed $200M globally ($137M domestically) in two weeks with a strong 34% second weekend box office drop. It became Amazon MGM Studios' most successful film ever, outperforming Creed III. Sequel discussions are already underway with author Andy Weir, and the film's success provides momentum for Amazon's upcoming Bond film project.
item.keywords: Project Hail Mary box office, $200M global revenue, Amazon MGM Studios, film sequel, Andy Weir

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [3/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: All original xAI co-founders except Elon Musk have departed, with Ross Nordeen being the latest to leave. This represents a common pattern in AI labs where founding teams disperse. Despite Nordeen's deep ties to Musk (Tesla, Twitter acquisition), the talent exodus highlights that investments were bets on Musk's vision rather than the team itself. xAI has received SpaceX shares as compensation but faces pressure to deliver results.
item.keywords: xAI co-founders departure, Ross Nordeen, Elon Musk, AI lab talent turnover, SpaceX equity compensation

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [2/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: ChatGPT's app store continues to struggle with adoption, repeating the failure of the custom GPTs directory from two years prior. Users find it faster and easier to use native apps and websites directly—booking through Booking.com or Uber's app—rather than through ChatGPT. Enterprise integrations and potential future device ecosystems remain the main strategic rationale, though past device efforts by Amazon have also underperformed.
item.keywords: ChatGPT apps, app store adoption, custom GPTs, enterprise integration, OpenAI devices

item.title: The Fall in the House of Sora [1/8]
item.author: "Spyglass Afterthoughts..." <mgsiegler@substack.com>
item.description: OpenAI has discontinued its Sora video generation product, ending a major push into video AI. The service burned $1M per day and required separate model training distinct from their LLM efforts. Key personnel disputes with Meta over talent hiring and the focus needed for their 'Spud' model contributed to the decision. The discontinuation was poorly communicated to Disney, who received less than an hour's notice before the plug was pulled.
item.keywords: OpenAI Sora shutdown, video generation AI, compute resource allocation, Disney partnership, AI model training costs

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [9/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Multiple major events scheduled for 2026 include: Mexico's San Marcos Fair (April 18-May 3), FIFA World Cup (June 11-July 19 across US/Canada/Mexico with US games on July 4), Edinburgh Festival Fringe (August 7-31), Munich Oktoberfest (Sept 19-Oct 4), Thailand lantern festivals (Nov 24-25), and Sydney New Year's Eve celebrations.
item.keywords: 2026 events, FIFA World Cup 2026, Oktoberfest, Edinburgh Festival, global events calendar

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [8/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Feast of the Black Nazarene in Manila, Philippines attracts almost 10 million devotees annually for a 30-hour procession featuring a replica of a 17th-century wooden statue of Jesus. Hasidic Jews gather in Uman, Ukraine for Rosh Hashanah with up to 60,000 believers attending the pilgrimage at Rabbi Nachman's tomb, even continuing after Russia's 2022 invasion.
item.keywords: Feast of Black Nazarene, Manila pilgrimage, Uman Ukraine, Rosh Hashanah, religious sites

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [7/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Religious pilgrimages drive massive global travel and economic activity, generating nearly $290 billion in economic activity as of 2024. The 2025 Hajj in Saudi Arabia drew over 1.6 million pilgrims paying $5,000-$25,000 per person. The Maha Kumbh Mela festival drew over 660 million people last year, making it the world's largest human gathering.
item.keywords: religious pilgrimage, Hajj, Maha Kumbh Mela, religious tourism, economic impact

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [6/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Sturgis Motorcycle Rally in South Dakota attracts approximately 500,000 people annually to a town of 7,000 residents. The 2025 rally generated about $1.6 million in state and local taxes, up 13% from 2024. However, rally expenses have consistently exceeded revenue in recent years.
item.keywords: Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, South Dakota tourism, event revenue, local taxes

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [5/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: F1 promoter fees accounted for approximately $824 million of F1's 2025 earnings. The Belgian Grand Prix contributes around $250 million annually to Belgium's economy. However, cancellation of two Middle Eastern races (Bahrain and Saudi Arabia) due to Iran conflict could cost F1 about $200 million in revenue.
item.keywords: F1 promoter fees, race cancellation, Belgium Grand Prix, Middle East races

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [4/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Formula 1 generated $3.9 billion in revenue in 2025, a 14% increase from 2024. The 2025 season was the most-attended F1 season ever with 6.7 million spectators across 24 races, 19 events sold out, 11 races set attendance records, and the British Grand Prix drew 500,000 fans.
item.keywords: Formula 1 revenue, F1 attendance records, motorsports tourism, British Grand Prix

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [3/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: California will host multiple major sporting events generating billions in local revenue: Super Bowl and NBA All-Star Game already occurred, FIFA World Cup games coming summer 2026, another Super Bowl in 2027, and Olympics in 2028. The 49ers CEO claimed the recent Super Bowl brought ~$500 million to the Bay Area.
item.keywords: California events, Super Bowl, FIFA World Cup, Olympics 2028, sports tourism revenue

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [2/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: BTS announced a reunion international tour, causing Booking.com searches to surge 6,700% for a November tour date in Taiwan. Flights and hotel rooms in 34 cities around the world sold out quickly following the announcement.
item.keywords: BTS reunion tour, K-pop, event travel, booking surge, music tour

item.title: ☕ Event Travel Brew [1/9]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Event travel is driving significant economic growth globally. Music tourism could reach $9 billion by 2030 (50% increase from 2023), while sports tourism is projected to exceed $1 trillion in coming years. Taylor Swift's and Beyoncé's tours demonstrated massive economic benefits for host cities through restaurant and hotel revenue.
item.keywords: event travel, music tourism, sports tourism, economic impact, concert tours

item.title: DCB #194｜The CCP’s latest “Ethnic Unity Law” creates ethnic division and confrontation. [3/3]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: During China's 2026 'Two Sessions,' rural elderly pension support emerged as a key issue. NPC deputy Lei Maoduan proposed raising basic pensions for farmers aged 70 and above to 500 yuan monthly over three years, addressing the current inadequate payment of just over 100 yuan per month. Scholars suggest combining higher age-based subsidies with strengthened social safety net support for vulnerable rural elderly, particularly the oldest-old demographic.
item.keywords: rural pensions, elderly care China, pension reform 2026, social welfare, aging population

item.title: DCB #194｜The CCP’s latest “Ethnic Unity Law” creates ethnic division and confrontation. [2/3]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: The European Union introduced the 'Industrial Accelerator Act' imposing higher foreign investment thresholds in four sectors: batteries, electric vehicles, photovoltaics, and critical raw materials. The policy uses technology transfer requirements, equity restrictions, and localization mandates to reshape market rules under 'EU manufacturing first' principle. For Chinese companies, this shifts competition from price-based to institutional compliance thresholds, forcing deeper localization while benefiting firms already established in Europe.
item.keywords: EU Industrial Accelerator Act, foreign investment restrictions, green manufacturing, supply chain localization, China trade barriers

item.title: DCB #194｜The CCP’s latest “Ethnic Unity Law” creates ethnic division and confrontation. [1/3]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: China's newly adopted 'Law on Promoting Ethnic Unity and Progress' is criticized as a tool for forced ethnic assimilation rather than genuine unity. The law shifts ethnic policy from administrative measures to legal enforcement, introducing four problematic provisions: replacing bilingual education with Chinese-language supremacy (Articles 15, 22), forcing cultural identity through law (Article 3's 'five identifications'), promoting compulsory mixed residential integration (Article 28), and enabling extraterritorial enforcement against overseas critics (Article 64). Critics argue these rigid legal frameworks will intensify ethnic conflict rather than promote stability.
item.keywords: China ethnic policy, Ethnic Unity Law 2026, linguistic assimilation, minority rights, cultural identity enforcement

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [6/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: China's 2026 GDP growth target of 4.5-5% represents a deliberate policy choice rather than a growth slowdown, providing flexibility for structural adjustment, risk prevention, and external shocks. The target maintains strong global positioning while shifting emphasis from speed to quality, incorporating employment, price stability, and livelihood improvements. The range allows regional differentiation while maintaining growth as employment foundation, embodying a 'worst-case preparation, best-case effort' approach.
item.keywords: GDP growth target 2026, economic policy flexibility, high-quality development, structural adjustment, regional differentiation, employment stability, macroeconomic management

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [5/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: China's food delivery subsidy wars, involving billions in platform spending, have failed to create genuine prosperity despite short-term traffic stimulus. The result is across-the-board value destruction: restaurants face increased volume without profit gains and accelerating closures; suppliers experience forced price cuts; consumers will eventually pay higher prices and face quality decline and food safety risks. The subsidy competition exemplifies market failure where cross-business profit leverage creates a prisoners' dilemma, eroding profitability and reinforcing deflationary expectations.
item.keywords: delivery platform subsidies, anti-monopoly regulation, market distortion, restaurant profitability, supply chain compression, consumer risk, platform competition

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [4/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: The 2026 National People's Congress highlighted unprecedented public focus on raising farmer pension payments, though the actual increase of only 20 yuan disappointed expectations. Despite limited policy breakthrough, the incident represents significant shifts in public discourse: multiple representatives vocalized support, mainstream media unusually criticized the measure as insufficient, and social consensus on the need for substantial increases (300-1000 yuan range within 3-5 years) has solidified, creating a positive feedback loop between media, experts, and policy discussions.
item.keywords: farmer pension increase, public discourse, Two Sessions 2026, social consensus, media coverage, welfare expansion, income distribution

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [3/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: Rural pension security in China faces significant challenges as elderly farmers, particularly those over 70, receive only 100+ yuan monthly, forcing many to continue high-intensity labor. A National People's Congress representative proposed raising basic pension to 500 yuan monthly over three years. The underlying issue is systemic: rural pensions depend on government subsidies with low absolute amounts and slow growth, creating widening gaps with urban pensions and constraining consumption and risk resilience.
item.keywords: rural pension reform, elderly welfare, basic pension 500 yuan, urban-rural disparity, social security, aging population, 2026 Two Sessions

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [2/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: The EU's Industrial Accelerator Act introduces technology transfer restrictions, equity limitations, and localization requirements for foreign investment in batteries, electric vehicles, photovoltaics, and critical raw materials. The policy reshapes market rules through public procurement integration and supply chain security measures, attempting to make low-carbon manufacturing commercially viable in Europe while reorienting global industrial division of labor.
item.keywords: EU Industrial Accelerator Act, foreign direct investment restrictions, technology transfer requirements, localization mandates, green manufacturing, supply chain security, China export competitiveness

item.title: DCB #194｜中共最新《民族團結法》實為製造民族分裂與對立 [1/6]
item.author: Dialogue China <dc8964@97296837.mailchimpapp.com>
item.description: China's new Ethnic Unity and Progress Promotion Law (民族團結進步促進法), passed at the March 2026 National People's Congress, is analyzed as effectively a forced assimilation law rather than a genuine unity measure. The law establishes four major problematic mechanisms: replacing bilingual policies with Han Chinese language dominance in education and public spaces; embedding political ideology (including CCP loyalty) into legal requirements with vague enforcement boundaries; forcing mixed residential patterns between Han and ethnic minorities; and granting cross-border enforcement authority against foreign individuals or organizations critical of China's ethnic policies.
item.keywords: Ethnic Unity Law 2026, Han language dominance, forced assimilation, ethnic minority rights, China National People's Congress, cultural autonomy, cross-border enforcement

item.description: François Chollet discusses a different approach to AGI beyond scaling, focusing on understanding intelligence from first principles. He launched ARC V3, a new benchmark designed to measure the ability to learn, adapt, and reason efficiently in entirely new environments, arguing that current systems may be hitting limits and true general intelligence may require fundamentally different approaches.

item.author: This Week at YC <info@ycombinator.com>
item.description: Solugen, a YC company, is reinventing chemical manufacturing by using biology and chemistry to produce chemicals from sugar instead of fossil fuels. Co-founders Gaurab Chakrabarti and Sean Hunt grew the company from a $7,000 PVC reactor to a billion-dollar valuation while competing with industry giants in the $6 trillion chemical manufacturing industry. Their approach combines enzymatic and catalytic production methods.
item.keywords: Solugen, chemical manufacturing, bio-based chemicals, fossil fuel alternatives, enzymatic production

item.keywords: Trump, Iran negotiations, TACO trade, crude oil, $110 per barrel, geopolitical

item.title: Rep. Letlow Bought DRS. Then Two Pentagon Contracts Followed. [1/6]

item.description: Rep. Julia Letlow (R-LA) purchased Leonardo DRS stock on February 2nd, and weeks later DRS was selected for two major Pentagon contract vehicles: the SHIELD missile defense IDIQ with a $151B ceiling and the ATSP5 rapid tech program with a $25B ceiling (shared among 13 contractors). Letlow is the only member of Congress who traded DRS stock. Her office states the trades were broker-managed by Merrill Lynch. DRS stock has risen 14% since her purchase.

Existing foreign-made models remain allowed, and exemptions are available through the Defense Department with domestic manufacturing plans.

item.title: ☕️ Miami exodus [1/13]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: US population growth slowed to 1.8 million people between July 2024 and July 2025, one of the lowest rates in history. The slowdown was driven by a rapid fall in international migration due to President Trump's crackdown on undocumented immigrants and federal policy changes. Large metro areas and counties along the US-Mexico border were hit hardest, with 1,270 counties losing residents.
item.keywords: US population growth, immigration policy, Trump border crackdown, Census Bureau, net migration

item.title: Donald Trump 延長炸伊朗電廠，究竟是再次TACO，還是緩兵之計?
item.author: 中環十一少 <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: President Trump announced a 10-day delay in military strikes against Iranian energy facilities, postponing action from the original deadline to April 6 at 8 PM Eastern Time. Trump stated the delay was at Iran's request and claimed negotiations are progressing "very smoothly." This marks the third revision of the ultimatum timeline within 7 days, following earlier deadlines of 48 hours and 5 days.
item.keywords: Trump Iran military strike delay, Iranian energy facilities, US-Iran negotiations, April 6 deadline, military escalation

item.title: 【訂戶優先睇】 Money Discord｜中東局勢反轉再反轉 傳統避險資產為何反而成為市場重災區？ [2/2]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Facing simultaneous pressures from surging consumer inflation and persistently high interest rates in 2026, control over energy resources is positioned as a critical factor for capital preservation in uncertain economic times. The email argues that energy security will be a key determinant of financial resilience amid broader economic turmoil.
item.keywords: consumer price inflation, high interest rates, energy control, capital preservation, economic uncertainty, 2026 outlook

item.title: 【訂戶優先睇】 Money Discord｜中東局勢反轉再反轉 傳統避險資產為何反而成為市場重災區？ [1/2]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Middle East geopolitical tensions have caused sharp reversals in oil and stock prices this week. Traditional safe-haven assets like gold, silver, and government bonds have failed to provide their expected hedge functions, with deleveraging triggering widespread asset price contraction. The analysis suggests that as Federal Reserve rate cut expectations fade and AI-driven corporate layoffs continue, energy supply competition at the bottom of economic and industrial chains will intensify in 2026.
item.keywords: Middle East geopolitical tensions, oil price volatility, safe-haven assets failure, gold silver government bonds, deleveraging, Federal Reserve rate cuts, AI layoffs, energy supply 2026

item.description: The UK government has criticized the Hong Kong government for cross-border suppression and called for the release of Jimmy Lai (黎智英). This statement reflects ongoing UK concerns about Hong Kong's autonomy and human rights conditions.
item.keywords: UK government, Hong Kong, Jimmy Lai, cross-border suppression, national security

item.title: 焦點22:30｜27.03.2026｜宏福苑居民聯署爭取終獲安排上樓、倫敦經貿辦國安案衞志樑爆「師父」身份、英批港府跨境打壓促放黎智英 [2/3]

item.keywords: Hongfu Garden, Hong Kong housing, resident petition, relocation

item.title: The Quest to Revive a Frozen Brain [5/5]
item.author: WSJ The Future of Everything <access@interactive.wsj.com>
item.description: NASA is preparing a budget request to Congress that includes plans for a $20 billion moon base and several new initiatives. The agency is pursuing "extraordinarily difficult" missions including lunar infrastructure development and the use of nuclear power in space.
item.keywords: NASA moon base, space budget, nuclear power in space, lunar exploration, Congress funding

item.title: The Quest to Revive a Frozen Brain [4/5]
item.author: WSJ The Future of Everything <access@interactive.wsj.com>
item.description: Fannie Mae is accepting crypto-backed mortgages for the first time through a partnership between mortgage company Better Home & Finance and crypto exchange Coinbase Global. The product allows home buyers to pledge cryptocurrency as collateral when obtaining Fannie Mae-backed mortgages.
item.keywords: crypto mortgages, Fannie Mae, Coinbase, blockchain lending, cryptocurrency collateral

item.title: The Quest to Revive a Frozen Brain [3/5]
item.author: WSJ The Future of Everything <access@interactive.wsj.com>
item.description: A new cement-based battery technology draws from ancient Roman concrete formulas and stores heat instead of electricity. This innovation aims to reduce reliance on fossil fuels by leveraging a 2,000-year-old scientific principle in modern energy storage.
item.keywords: cement battery, Roman concrete, heat storage, sustainable energy, fossil fuel reduction

item.title: The Quest to Revive a Frozen Brain [2/5]
item.author: WSJ The Future of Everything <access@interactive.wsj.com>
item.description: AI-powered journalism is rapidly transforming media production. Nick Lichtenberg at Fortune has generated 600 stories since rejoining the publication in July using AI technology, signaling a major shift in how the media industry may operate going forward.
item.keywords: AI journalism, Fortune magazine, automated content generation, media industry transformation

item.title: The Quest to Revive a Frozen Brain [1/5]
item.author: WSJ The Future of Everything <access@interactive.wsj.com>
item.description: German researchers successfully froze mouse brain tissue at minus 196 degrees Celsius using liquid nitrogen and cryopreservation chemicals, then thawed it up to seven days later. The neurons not only survived but remained functional and able to exchange electrical signals again. This advances brain preservation from maintaining structure to preserving function, with potential applications for drug testing, studying neurodegenerative diseases, and eventually preserving organs or bodies.
item.keywords: brain tissue cryopreservation, mouse neurons, functional preservation, liquid nitrogen freezing, neuroprotection

item.title: ICYMI: Duplicate posts, customize your page & handle refunds faster [2/2]
item.author: Patreon for Creators <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Patreon is announcing product improvements and updates for creators, including features related to duplicate posts, page customization, and faster refund handling to make daily platform use easier.
item.keywords: Patreon product updates, duplicate posts, page customization, refund handling, creator features

item.title: ICYMI: Duplicate posts, customize your page & handle refunds faster [1/2]
item.author: Patreon for Creators <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Patreon is updating the sender address for new post emails from creators. Starting soon, post emails will come from patreonforcreators@creator.patreon.com instead of the previous address, as part of improvements to how creators' emails display in subscribers' inboxes.
item.keywords: Patreon, email sender address, creator emails, inbox delivery

item.title: 1000個移英港人做了一件事 感動整個英國｜離散港情
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Over 1,000 Hong Kong migrants in the UK have participated in a crochet initiative started by 5 Hong Kong mothers. Within one year, the project expanded to 40 regions with over 1,000 volunteers who hand-crafted 30,000 items, raising over £23,000 for UK charities. The initiative has positively impacted sick children, lonely elderly people, and bereaved families.
item.keywords: Hong Kong migrants UK, crochet charity initiative, volunteer community project, £23, 000 fundraising, 30, 000 handmade items, diaspora community

item.keywords: Nigella Lawson, FT columnist, Eat My Words, cooking column, food writing

item.title: Weekend highlight: Pernod Ricard and Jack Daniel’s owner in talks to combine operations [1/4]
item.author: Financial Times <stories-features@mail.ft.com>
item.description: Pernod Ricard and Brown-Forman (owner of Jack Daniel's) are in discussions to combine their operations and create a premium alcohol group. The talks come as the spirits sector faces subdued demand.

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [10/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Meta laid off hundreds of employees, including staff in its Reality Labs division, as the company prioritizes spending on AI development.
item.keywords: Meta layoffs, Reality Labs, AI spending, workforce reduction

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [9/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The US Postal Service is increasing prices by 8% to cover rising fuel costs. Additionally, President Trump will travel to China in May to meet with Xi Jinping, delaying the trip to focus on the war in Iran.
item.keywords: USPS price increase, postal rates, Trump China visit, Xi Jinping meeting

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [8/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Clear airport security app has been downloaded 289,000 times since the beginning of the month following TSA staffing shortages caused by a government shutdown over ICE funding. This represents more than 3x the downloads from a year ago, with Clear's stock rising about 60% over the past month, while airports like Houston's Bush Intercontinental faced security lines stretching up to four hours.
item.keywords: TSA shortage, government shutdown, Clear app, airport security, airport delays

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [7/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: A TikTok account called Fruit Love Island, which created AI-generated parodies of the reality show starring anthropomorphic fruit, was removed from the platform after reaching 3.3 million followers and viral success. The channel launched earlier in the month with episodes generating millions of views before being deleted, though copies and parodies continue on other platforms.
item.keywords: Fruit Love Island, TikTok removal, AI-generated content, viral trend, ai.cinema021

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [6/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The NBA's board of governors voted unanimously to expand the league by adding franchises in Las Vegas and Seattle for the 2028-29 season, becoming the 31st and 32nd teams. New franchises are expected to pay $7 billion to $10 billion for entry, though the timing is complicated by a $1 million salary cap shortfall due to declining local media revenue.
item.keywords: NBA expansion, Las Vegas, Seattle, 2028-29 season, $7-10 billion franchise fee

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [5/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Stephen Colbert will co-write a new Lord of the Rings film titled 'The Lord of the Rings: Shadow of the Past' with his son and a writer from previous films in the series, currently in development with Warner Bros. This project arrives after the announcement that The Late Show will be canceled in May.
item.keywords: Stephen Colbert, Lord of the Rings, Shadow of the Past, Warner Bros, film development

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [4/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Supreme Court unanimously ruled that Cox Communications cannot be held liable for users' music piracy, overturning a case that previously involved a $1 billion judgment. Justice Clarence Thomas wrote that companies are not liable for copyright infringement merely by providing a service knowing it will be used by some to infringe copyrights, as long as they don't encourage illegal activity.
item.keywords: Supreme Court ruling, Cox Communications, music piracy, copyright liability, internet providers

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [3/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Iran rejected the Trump administration's 15-point ceasefire proposal and countered with its own five-point proposal. The US proposal reportedly involved rolling back sanctions and addressing Iran's nuclear program, while Iran's counter-demands include reparations and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, conditions likely unacceptable to the US.
item.keywords: Iran ceasefire, Trump administration, US sanctions, nuclear program, Strait of Hormuz

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [2/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: In a separate case, a New Mexico jury ordered Meta to pay $375 million for failing to protect young users from sexually explicit content, trafficking, and online predators. State investigators conducted a sting operation using decoy accounts of minors that were reportedly overwhelmed with solicitations from predators. Meta plans to appeal this decision.
item.keywords: Meta child safety case, New Mexico verdict, online predators, child exploitation, $375 million fine

item.title: ☕️ Sloppy fruit [1/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Meta and YouTube lost a landmark social media addiction trial in California, with a jury finding them negligent for designing addictive features like infinite scroll that harmed adolescents. Meta was ordered to pay $4.2 million and YouTube $1.8 million to a 20-year-old plaintiff who claimed addiction to Instagram and YouTube intensified her depression. This bellwether case is significant because Section 230 protections did not shield the platforms from liability, as plaintiffs focused on platform design rather than user-generated content.
item.keywords: Meta lawsuit, YouTube addiction trial, social media harm, infinite scroll, Section 230, California verdict

item.title: 焦點22:30｜26.03.2026｜宏福苑聽證會爆黃碧嬌涉偽造授權票、涉售黎智英傳一拳書館四人已獲保釋、袁松彪推翻口供終認跨境追數中間人 [3/3]

item.description: Yuan Songbiao reversed his previous testimony and finally admitted to acting as an intermediary in cross-border debt collection activities. His change of testimony represents a significant development in an ongoing case involving unauthorized cross-border financial operations.
item.keywords: Yuan Songbiao, testimony reversal, cross-border debt collection, intermediary

item.title: Inside: Child Care, Due Process, Government Land [3/3]
item.author: "Stossel in the Classroom" <custsvc@stosselintheclassroom.org>
item.description: The federal government owns nearly one-third of all U.S. land, including more than half of the land in five states. The discussion examines how that land is currently used and considers arguments for selling portions of it, balancing concerns about housing, conservation, environmental protection, and the tradeoffs between public versus private ownership and management.
item.keywords: federal land ownership, public lands, property rights, conservation, environmental protection, land development

item.title: Inside: Child Care, Due Process, Government Land [2/3]
item.author: "Stossel in the Classroom" <custsvc@stosselintheclassroom.org>
item.description: Civil asset forfeiture allows police to seize private property without charging someone with a crime. Originally intended to target drug trafficking, the practice has expanded far beyond its original purpose, raising significant questions about due process rights and property rights protections.
item.keywords: civil asset forfeiture, due process, property rights, police seizure, drug trafficking

item.title: Inside: Child Care, Due Process, Government Land [1/3]
item.author: "Stossel in the Classroom" <custsvc@stosselintheclassroom.org>
item.description: New York political leaders have proposed expanding taxpayer-funded child care. Supporters point to increased opportunities for families, while critics raise concerns about costs, incentives, and past program results. The Both Sides of the Issue content explores competing perspectives on what 'free' means in practice, who benefits, and potential unintended consequences. This marks the 149th installment in the Both Sides series, with a 150th milestone announcement expected.
item.keywords: universal child care, taxpayer-funded programs, New York policy, subsidies, incentives

item.title: The Stores Edition: Gulf clap [5/5]
item.author: "Retail Brew" <crew@community.morningbrew.com>
item.description: A featured resource discusses how retailers can leverage private label marketing advantages over national brands as private label sales continue to grow. The resource outlines effective strategies to drive consumers to switch their grocery purchases.
item.keywords: private label, retail marketing, national brands, consumer switching, grocery

item.title: The Stores Edition: Gulf clap [4/5]
item.author: "Retail Brew" <crew@community.morningbrew.com>
item.description: The US is increasingly relying on tariffs as its primary trade tool as the WTO becomes sidelined and issues like forced tech transfers and IP theft persist. This represents a shift from decades of maintaining some of the world's lowest tariffs and could reshape consumer purchasing patterns.
item.keywords: tariffs, US trade policy, WTO, tech transfers, IP theft, consumer prices

item.title: The Stores Edition: Gulf clap [3/5]
item.author: "Retail Brew" <crew@community.morningbrew.com>
item.description: Michaels has joined Target in cutting prices on thousands of items. Both retailers are signaling that price reductions are winding down or will be less of a focus in the year ahead.
item.keywords: Michaels, Target, price cuts, discounting strategy, retail pricing

item.title: The Stores Edition: Gulf clap [2/5]
item.author: "Retail Brew" <crew@community.morningbrew.com>
item.description: Dollar General is introducing a new, more open store format designed to create a 'treasure hunt' shopping experience. The discount chain is expanding beyond its core low-cost essentials business to offer more discretionary goods.
item.keywords: Dollar General, store format, treasure hunt experience, discretionary goods, discount retail

item.title: The Stores Edition: Gulf clap [1/5]
item.author: "Retail Brew" <crew@community.morningbrew.com>
item.description: Regional retailers in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain are facing uncertainty as geopolitical tensions threaten to dampen discretionary spending. Shopping in these Gulf countries remains heavily mall-based, making retailers particularly vulnerable to disruption.
item.keywords: Gulf retail, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, geopolitical tensions, discretionary spending

item.description: Apple confirmed its Maps app will begin displaying advertisements starting this summer.

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [8/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Meta was ordered to pay $375 million in damages after a New Mexico jury found the company violated state law by failing to protect users from child predators.
item.keywords: Meta, child safety, lawsuit, New Mexico, damages

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [7/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: US reality television is in decline, with unscripted series premieres dropping 15% last year and by a third since 2022. All genres including cooking, crime, home design, and travel shows are declining. Despite being cheaper to produce than scripted content, reality TV is affected by media consolidation and declining ratings.
item.keywords: reality TV decline, unscripted series, television industry, ratings

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [6/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Researchers at CERN successfully transported 92 antiprotons via truck in a specially designed tank, completing a 30-minute test drive around campus. This is the first step toward an eight-hour transport to Heinrich Heine University in Düsseldorf by 2029. The transport demonstrates capability to study antimatter, which could explain why the universe is dominated by matter rather than antimatter.
item.keywords: CERN, antimatter transport, antiprotons, Heinrich Heine University, Large Hadron Collider

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [5/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: United Airlines announced fleet upgrades for 250 new airplanes including more premium seats, lie-flat seats on transcontinental flights, and the new 'Relax Row' offering three economy seats as a bed. Premium revenue jumped 11% last year while basic economy fell 5%. CEO Scott Kirby warned ticket prices may increase 20% if jet fuel costs remain elevated due to Iran conflict.
item.keywords: United Airlines, Boeing 787-9, premium seats, Relax Row, ticket prices, Iran war

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [4/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Epic Games announced layoffs of 1,000 employees due to declining engagement and spending exceeding revenue. CEO Tim Sweeney stated the company needs major cuts to remain funded. Epic was valued at over $30 billion as of 2024.
item.keywords: Epic Games, layoffs, Tim Sweeney, engagement decline, Fortnite

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [3/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Senate and White House are nearing a deal to fund the Department of Homeland Security to address TSA staffing shortages causing airport delays. The DHS has been shut down since February. Democrats have blocked ICE funding until reforms are made following killings of two US citizens by immigration enforcement officers in Minneapolis.
item.keywords: DHS funding, TSA staffing, airport delays, ICE budget, government shutdown

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [2/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Pentagon plans to deploy 3,000 soldiers from the US Army's 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East to support operations against Iran, according to the Wall Street Journal. Pakistan has offered to mediate US-Iran talks, while Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman is reportedly pushing Trump to continue the war.
item.keywords: Pentagon deployment, 82nd Airborne Division, Iran, Middle East, Pakistan mediation, Saudi Arabia

item.title: ☕ Public freakout [1/12]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Private credit funds Apollo Global Management and Ares are limiting shareholder payouts as investors rush to withdraw funds. Moody's downgraded a KKR and Future Standard private credit fund to junk status after borrowers defaulted. The $1.8 trillion private credit industry faces stress from significant defaults and concerns that AI disruption could impact software companies, which represent 30% of its loan portfolio.
item.keywords: private credit crisis, Apollo Global Management, Ares, fund withdrawals, Moody's downgrade, KKR, defaults

item.title: 焦點22:30｜25.03.2026｜禁書紅線未明確獨立書店稱無法判斷、袁松彪被控方盤問窮追猛打焫着跳掣、廉署夥競爭事務委員會搗破圍標集團 [1/3]
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: Hong Kong independent bookstores express difficulty determining boundaries regarding banned books, as regulations remain unclear. Store owners cannot assess what publications may violate restrictions, creating uncertainty in their operations.
item.keywords: banned books, Hong Kong, independent bookstores, regulations, censorship

item.title: 支聯會案．海外證詞｜高志活為鄒幸彤及支聯會作見證
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: High志活 (Jens Gals) provided overseas testimony for defendant 鄒幸彤 (Zou Xingcong) and the 支聯會 (Hong Kong Alliance in Support of Democratic Movements in China) in a legal case. About a year prior, 鄒幸彤 invited people who participated in June 4th movements, assemblies, and 支聯會 activities to submit written testimonies with the theme "In the struggle between memory and rewriting memory...our unfinished story." The publication 《追光者》 (Light Chaser) has been authorized to republish the full text of these testimonies.
item.keywords: 支聯會 case testimony, 鄒幸彤 defendant, overseas witness testimony, June 4th movement, Hong Kong Alliance, court case

item.title: Sneak peek #4: 錢 vs 夢想？CanMen點睇錢、點樣處理分歧、點樣隔空合作搞YouTube！｜CanMen JM9團隊哲學
item.author: 住加男人－CanMen (via Patreon) <bingo@updates.patreon.com>
item.description: CanMen, a Patreon-based content creator, is sharing an exclusive paid-member post for free as a thank-you to new members. The post is titled 'Sneak peek #4: 錢 vs 夢想？CanMen點睇錢、點樣處理分歧、點樣隔空合作搞YouTube！｜CanMen JM9團隊哲學' and addresses topics including money versus dreams, how CanMen views finances, handling disagreements, remote collaboration for YouTube, and team philosophy for the JM9 group.
item.keywords: CanMen, Patreon exclusive content, money vs dreams, team collaboration, YouTube production, JM9 team philosophy

item.title: Sneak peek #4: 【訂戶優先睇】 京城外望｜從郭鳳儀父親被定罪看國安法的濫用
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News (via Patreon) <bingo@updates.patreon.com>
item.description: This email is a Patreon notification offering an exclusive paid-member post from 追光者 Pulse HK News for free to new members. The post title references 「京城外望｜從郭鳳儀父親被定罪看國安法的濫用」(Beijing Outlook: Examining National Security Law Abuse Through Kwok Feng-yi's Father's Conviction). The email appears incomplete with truncated HTML content.
item.keywords: National Security Law Hong Kong, Kwok Feng-yi, legal abuse allegations, Patreon exclusive, Hong Kong politics

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [8/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: The video 'Who Is Winning Southeast Asia's Chip Race?' on the Diplomat Asia YouTube channel examines which Southeast Asian countries are succeeding and which are falling behind in the global semiconductor competition.
item.keywords: Southeast Asia, semiconductor industry, chip manufacturing, technology competition

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [7/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: A podcast episode titled 'Myanmar, War, and Federalism' features Beyond the Mekong host Luke Hunt discussing the evolving civil war in Myanmar with Joe Lo Bianco, president of the Australia Myanmar Institute, covering the politics behind the conflict and prospects for a future federal government.
item.keywords: Myanmar, civil war, federalism, Australia Myanmar Institute, podcast

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [6/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: A 2022 corruption scandal involving former State Committee for National Security head Kamshybek Tashiev and Kyrgyzneftegaz has resurfaced four years later as political retribution. Tashiev's nephew has been arrested and Tashiev questioned as a witness; meanwhile, Makhabat Tazhibek kyzy, jailed director of outlet Temirov Live that originally reported the scandal, has been granted a new trial after two years imprisonment.
item.keywords: Kyrgyzstan, Kamshybek Tashiev, corruption scandal, Kyrgyzneftegaz, Temirov Live, political retribution

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [5/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: Southeast Asian governments are securing bilateral energy deals amid global supply crisis from Middle East conflict. Vietnam and Russia agreed on energy partnerships including Vietnam's first nuclear power plant construction; Singapore and Australia committed to maintaining open trade in oil and LNG; the Philippines will receive its first Russian oil shipment since 2021.
item.keywords: Southeast Asia, energy security, Vietnam-Russia nuclear, Singapore-Australia LNG, oil supplies, Middle East crisis

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [4/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: China's mediation efforts between Afghanistan and Pakistan have failed to produce results despite Beijing's strategic interest in both nations. Chinese special envoy Yu Xiaoyong visited Islamabad and Kabul in early March seeking a ceasefire, but the two countries, engaged in what Pakistan declared an 'open war' since February, have not come to the negotiating table.
item.keywords: China mediation, Afghanistan-Pakistan conflict, ceasefire, Yu Xiaoyong, South Asia geopolitics

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [3/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae successfully navigated her first White House visit on March 19 despite high stakes and Trump's demands for allies to join military operations in the Strait of Hormuz. She emerged unscathed by offering vague promises of unspecified support, allowing the summit to refocus on economic cooperation regarding critical minerals, energy, and Japanese investment in the U.S.
item.keywords: Japan, Trump, Takaichi Sanae, US-Japan summit, Strait of Hormuz, economic cooperation

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [2/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: Dr. Zhanibek Arynov from Nazarbayev University's Graduate School of Public Policy discusses public perceptions of the Eurasian Economic Union a decade after its launch. He notes that while core skepticism narratives remain unchanged, their intensity has increased compared to the early years of the union.
item.keywords: Eurasian Economic Union, EAEU, public opinion, Central Asia, Nazarbayev University, geopolitics

item.title: Why Bangladeshi Villagers Are Worried About the Iran-US War [1/8]
item.author: The Diplomat <newsletters@thediplomat.com>
item.description: Bangladeshi villagers are deeply anxious about the ongoing Iran-US war despite being thousands of kilometers away, as millions of families depend on income from workers in the Middle East. The case of Mohammad Azaharul Islam's family illustrates this concern—his son Rakib Hossain works at a petrol station in Saudi Arabia since December 2023 and is scheduled to return to Riyadh on April 14, making the distant conflict feel immediate and threatening to families at home.
item.keywords: Bangladesh, Iran-US war, Middle East conflict, migrant workers, Saudi Arabia, economic anxiety

item.title: OpenClaw agents can be guilt-tripped into self-sabotage
item.author: "Will Knight, WIRED" <wired@newsletters.wired.com>
item.description: Researchers at Northeastern University conducted a study where OpenClaw AI agents were invited to their lab, resulting in unexpected chaos. The research demonstrated that safety measures and good behavior programmed into powerful AI models can become vulnerabilities. In one example, researchers successfully manipulated an agent into disclosing secrets by using guilt—scolding it for sharing information about a user on the Moltbook AI-only social network.
item.keywords: OpenClaw agents, Northeastern University, AI vulnerability, guilt manipulation, AI safety, agent exploitation

item.title: 【訂戶優先睇】 橫流集｜後記：不沉默的人 鄒幸彤與知識人的典範
item.author: 追光者 Pulse HK News <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: A new book titled "默存與噤聲：威權時代的香港知識份子" (Silence and Silencing: Hong Kong Intellectuals in the Age of Authoritarianism) is being prepared for publication. The book collects eight articles previously published in "追光者" (Pulse HK News) that examine silence as a theme, including critiques of the Hong Kong Philosophy Purple Bauhinia International Forum and the Chinese University's dismissal of Kwan Ching-fung. The afterword uses pro-democracy activist Chow Hang-tung (鄒幸彤) as an exemplar of intellectuals who refuse silence and speak truth.
item.keywords: Hong Kong intellectuals, silence and resistance, Chow Hang-tung, authoritarian control, academic freedom, Chinese University dismissal

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [6/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: The author notes the Wang Fuk Court fire incident from November claimed 168 lives and displaced nearly two thousand families. Four months later, affected residents have not been allowed to return home to collect belongings, yet burglaries of units have occurred while law enforcement and workers have access, resulting in resident property theft.
item.keywords: Wang Fuk Court fire, 168 deaths, displaced families, property theft, government access abuse

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [5/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: The author cites multiple cases of Hong Kong Police Force misconduct, including a 2025 case involving 12 counts of unauthorized access to police systems, a 2018 incident where an exhibits officer allegedly stole HK$860,000, and another 2018 case of theft of HK$3,000 from a citizen's wallet, illustrating the risk of corruption when law enforcement gains access to digital credentials.
item.keywords: Hong Kong Police misconduct, officer unauthorized access, theft from evidence room, HK$860, 000, law enforcement corruption

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [4/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: The author argues that password disclosure under the new National Security Law provisions creates significant risks for self-custodied cryptocurrency and digital assets. Unlike physical theft with potential recovery, cryptocurrency theft is permanent and untraceable once sent to unauthorized wallets, with no safeguards mentioned in the implementation rules.
item.keywords: self-custodied cryptocurrency, password security, digital asset protection, irreversible theft, regulatory gap

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [3/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: The South Korean government accidentally published private keys of seized cold wallets online, leading to immediate theft of cryptocurrencies worth approximately 6.4 billion won (US$5 million). Similar incidents have occurred three times this year, involving amounts up to US$20 million, demonstrating risks of government mishandling of digital asset credentials.
item.keywords: South Korea cryptocurrency theft, cold wallet private keys, 6.4 billion won, digital asset security, government data breach

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [2/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: Book Punch, a bookstore, was raided by law enforcement and prosecuted for selling 'seditious publications' including a biography of Jimmy Lai. The founder Pong Yat-ming and three staff members were taken away. This incident exemplifies the practical impact of the new National Security Law amendments on privacy and personal communications.
item.keywords: Book Punch raid, Pong Yat-ming, seditious publications, Jimmy Lai biography, Hong Kong bookstore

item.title: The price of privacy? HK$100k and 1 year in prison. [1/6]
item.author: LikeCoin <likecoin@substack.com>
item.description: Hong Kong's National Security Law amendments, gazetted on Monday, grant law enforcement agencies power to compel disclosure of electronic device passwords or decryption methods. Refusal to comply is now a crime punishable by up to one year in prison and a fine of HK$100,000. These provisions were implemented with immediate effect, bypassing Legislative Council scrutiny.
item.keywords: Hong Kong National Security Law, password disclosure requirement, digital privacy, criminal penalty HK$100, 000, law enforcement power

item.title: 【Patreon Live Q&A 26/3】為甚麼伊朗代表願意與魯比奧談判？ / 中遠航運恢復中東訂倉 / 中投公司據報考慮重新投資美國資產
item.author: Victor Ng Ming Tak <bingo@patreon.com>
item.description: A Patreon Live Q&A session scheduled for March 26 will discuss why Iranian representatives are willing to negotiate with Rubio, China COSCO Shipping's resumption of Middle East bookings, and China Investment Corporation's reported consideration of reinvesting in U.S. assets.
item.keywords: Iran negotiations, Rubio, COSCO Shipping, Middle East, China Investment Corporation, U.S. assets

item.title: ☕ Bad bet [10/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Bill Cosby was hit with a $19 million jury verdict in a civil lawsuit brought by Donna Motsinger, who claims Cosby drugged and raped her in 1972.
item.keywords: Bill Cosby, civil lawsuit, jury verdict, sexual assault claim

item.title: ☕ Bad bet [9/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: The Trump administration reached an agreement with French energy giant TotalEnergies to terminate nearly $1 billion worth of offshore wind leases and instead invest in oil and natural gas production, with the US government reimbursing TotalEnergies for the terminated leases.
item.keywords: TotalEnergies, offshore wind, oil and gas, energy policy, lease termination

item.title: ☕ Bad bet [8/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Sen. Markwayne Mullin was confirmed as the next secretary of Homeland Security amid an agency funding standoff with ICE stationed at airports.
item.keywords: Markwayne Mullin, Homeland Security, Senate confirmation, ICE

item.title: ☕ Bad bet [7/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: A Cornell study found that workers susceptible to "corporate bullshit" are worse at their jobs, scoring lower on cognitive tests and workplace decision-making. Researchers created a "Corporate Bullshit Receptivity Scale" and found that high-scorers were more likely to view bosses as visionary while showing poorer analytic thinking.
item.keywords: corporate communication, cognitive study, workplace decision-making, employee performance, Cornell research

item.title: ☕ Bad bet [6/10]
item.author: Morning Brew <crew@morningbrew.com>
item.description: Samsung's pilot program introducing advertisements on high-end smart refrigerators (Family Hub fridges costing $1,800+) has triggered fierce customer backlash. Banner ads started appearing in October; customers can disable them but lose access to news, weather, and calendar widgets, while full-screen ads cannot be disabled.
item.keywords: Samsung, smart refrigerator, advertisements, customer backlash, Family Hub
